Through this project-based learning structure, students deepen their proficiency and develop sophisticated critical thinking by analyzing four complex units—Global Perspective and Cultural Diversity, Media and Pop Culture, Lifestyle and Well-being, and Customs and Traditions—which require the evaluation of conflicting cultural values, societal pressures on mental health, and generational relationship dynamics. The course’s rigorous framework emphasizes in-depth analysis, evidence-based argumentation, and advanced expression in listening, speaking, reading, and writing, ensuring readiness for university-level discourse.
prerequisites: Current teacher recommendation based on demonstrated proficiency and progress toward the exit target.

title: AT Chinese Language: Media & Society
description: The digital era has revolutionized traditional writing, merging diverse media forms in an interconnected world. This course serves as a bridge between various media, empowering students to create boundary-crossing works. The course has two main objectives: firstly, to critically analyze a range of non-literary texts— advertisements, broadcast, gaming, speeches, opinion pieces, movies, and social media posts—exploring their language features and persuasive techniques; secondly, to enhance writing skills across multiple genres, including interviews, feature stories, precision news writing, investigative journalism, and radio, TV, and internet news. Through engaging with current affairs and real-world contexts, students will develop an acute awareness of language in Chinese news and reportage. By the end of the course, students will master the art of crafting and publishing impactful news reports and advocacy stories grounded in well-conducted interviews.
prerequisites: Current teacher recommendation based on demonstrated proficiency and progress toward the exit target. All students new to SAS will be invited for a placement test to determine the most appropriate level.

title: Spanish: Novice
description: This course is for students who have little or no experience with the language. It provides students with the necessary skills to understand and create meaningful communication from early on in a supportive and rich environment. The course focuses on the development of listening and speaking through interpersonal communication. Novice speakers can manage a number of uncomplicated communicative tasks in straightforward social situations. They can express personal meaning by relying heavily on learned phrases (memorized language) or combinations of these. They also respond to simple, direct questions or requests for information. The skills of writing and interpretive listening and reading are also integrated into the course to the extent that they foster the development of students’ communicative ability as appropriate to the performance target.
prerequisites: None

title: Spanish: Intermediate
note: Dependent on enrollment numbers these courses may be scheduled concurrently with other intermediate classes of the same language. When a class is scheduled concurrently, students will receive differentiated instruction to meet their needs, will receive credit for the level of the class in which they are enrolled, and that is the course that will appear on their transcript. All students new to SAS will be invited for a placement test to determine the most appropriate level.
description: These courses are for students who have reached at least a Novice level of performance in interpersonal listening and speaking. title: Spanish: Intermediate High
note: Dependent on enrollment numbers these courses may be scheduled concurrently with other intermediate classes of the same language. description: These courses are for students who have demonstrated a solid Intermediate Mid level of proficiency in interpersonal listening and speaking. With differentiation and new culturally rich thematic units each year, teachers engage with and support students by meeting them where they are at in the proficiency building process. While this course continues to emphasize interpersonal listening and speaking, interpretive listening and reading, and presentational writing, these skills are more formally developed and assessed at the intermediate high level. Intermediate High speakers are able to successfully handle uncomplicated tasks and social situations requiring an exchange of information about their school, recreation, particular interests, and areas of competence. They also demonstrate an increasing ability to express their own ideas about some topics beyond themselves such as current events or matters of public and community interest. Students at this level also demonstrate the ability to resolve problems they might encounter in their daily lives. Intermediate High students will learn to narrate and describe in three major time frames - present, past, and future - mostly in connected paragraphs.
prerequisites: Current teacher recommendation based on demonstrated proficiency and progress toward the exit target.

title: AT Spanish Language: Contemporary Social Issues
note: All students new to SAS will be invited for a placement test to determine the most appropriate level.
description: This inquiry-based course invites students to strengthen and apply their Spanish language skills through the exploration of pressing social issues in the contemporary Spanish-speaking world. Using authentic sources such as art, journalism, and digital media, students will analyze how communities and individuals navigate complex topics including gender roles, machismo, ethics in science, and the influence of rhetoric and media manipulation. The course emphasizes the use of language as a tool for critical thought and social engagement, fostering both linguistic accuracy and intercultural awareness. Through projectbased learning and collaborative inquiry, students will develop their interpretive and presentational skills while exploring art in political contexts as a form of protest and global dialogue. Ultimately, the course aims to deepen students’ ability to express nuanced ideas in Spanish and to reflect thoughtfully on contemporary cultural and ethical dilemmas.
prerequisites: Current teacher recommendation based on demonstrated Intermediate High proficiency level and progress toward the exit target.

title: Art I: Foundations
description: In this survey course, students are exposed to a variety of media through study of the elements and principles of art and design. Students acquire and apply skills using a variety of media and techniques. Pencil, charcoal, colored pencil, various paints, sculptural materials and linoleum for printmaking are examples of media offered to students. Emphasis is placed on skills acquisition and creativity. This course enables students to identify their strengths and possible areas of interest for future development. It provides the necessary foundation for the more advanced Studio Art courses.
prerequisites: None

title: Ceramics I
description: In this one-semester course, students learn basic hand building, decorating and glazing techniques. Students are free to develop their own ideas within structured guidelines while building on acquired skills. Students leave the class with an assortment of forms of different functions, shapes, and sizes. Instructional time is also spent on sculptural pieces. Students are responsible for preparing their materials and looking after their pieces through the various stages of the ceramic process from construction through glazing. An introduction to glaze chemistry will be given as a part of this course but will not be the focus. This course is a prerequisite for students who wish to continue on to learn potter’s wheel techniques in Ceramics II.
prerequisites: None

title: Wind Ensemble
description: Wind Ensemble is a very advanced band course for serious musicians who wish to challenge their skills with more difficult music. They play a varied repertoire of classical and popular music and will be encouraged to do solo and small ensemble performances as well. The Wind Ensemble represents the school in the community through concerts and programs. All Wind Ensemble members are required to attend all scheduled dress rehearsals, performances, including after school, evenings or weekends.
prerequisites: Audition

title: Symphonic Band
description: Symphonic Band is an audition-based group for intermediate to advanced musicians who wish to challenge their skills with more difficult music. The band will perform in four major concerts during the year, playing a variety of musical styles ranging from popular to classical. All Symphonic Band members are required to attend all scheduled dress rehearsals, performances, including after school or weekends.
prerequisites: Audition or completion of Concert Band with a B or higher.

title: Theater: Production - Acting
description: The ensemble is the foundation supporting all our work in theater. Students engage in a wide variety of ensemble and theater exercises throughout the semester. They develop fundamental acting skills through scene work and acting exercises. Students also begin the process of creating original work. This class can be used as the prerequisite for Advanced Production.
prerequisites: None

title: Theater: Advanced Production
description: Students work in small and full-class ensembles creating and rehearsing original pieces of theater. This work is presented as part of the Theatre Festival in April, so there is a minimal amount of after-school rehearsal required. This course is the prerequisite for the Advanced Topic Performing Arts course.
prerequisites: Any theater course.

title: Theater: Improvisation
description: Contrary to popular belief, Improvisation performers do not just ‘make it all up’ on the spot. There are skills and structures providing the springboard for entertaining improvisation. Students will develop these skills and performance forms in a workshop process emphasizing collaboration and play. This course can serve as the prerequisite for Advanced Improvisation and Advanced Production.
prerequisites: None

title: Robotics Science
description: This course applies learning in the areas of mechanics, electronics, CAD, and robotics design. Students will investigate criteria and constraints to integrate robotic solutions into realworld problems, applying a hands-on, iterative approach to the projects. By combining technical skills with research and collaboration, students will develop creative solutions and refine them through testing and feedback. Class projects will be shared with an authentic audience, and successful students will excel in collaboration, innovation, and perseverance.
prerequisites: None

title: Business
note: Offered in 2026-27. May not be offered in 2027-28.
description: This course will explore the world of modern business through project-based learning. The course will guide students through the essential activities of an enterprise, including finance and accounting, human resources, operations, and marketing. Students will become critical thinkers, analyzing, discussing, and solving realworld business case problems. Students also improve their written and oral communication skills in authentic settings when reporting their solutions to business cases. Students will polish their technology skills by authentically using computer skills as business people would: preparing presentations, calculating, preparing, and analyzing quantitative data in Excel, and creating marketing materials using image and video manipulation tools. The course is designed for those who would like a better understanding of the world of commerce or hope to one day join the business field.
prerequisites: None

title: Personal Finance: You and Your Money
note: Not offered in 2026-27. May be offered in 2027-28.
description: Few high school and college graduates are financially literate when they first enter the workforce. This course gives students an advantage in the real world by developing their financial literacy. Students will learn that high salaries don’t guarantee future wealth unless earnings are properly managed. Students will learn to manage their money through responsible spending and investing habits. In this course students will track their own daily spending from the first day of the class and explore the merits of careful consumption and effective investing through a series of project-based discoveries.
prerequisites: None

title: AT Entrepreneurship
description: Entrepreneurship provides real world, hands-on learning on what it’s like to actually start a company. The goal is to give students a framework to test the business model of a startup while creating all of the pressures and demands of the real world in an early stage start-up. They will learn marketing, finance, and other business disciplines, while also acquiring organizational skills such as time management and leadership development. The class is also a vehicle to develop character, intellect, and resilience in students. Students start the semester with an immediate immersion into the Lean Startup methodology adopted from University of California Berkeley and Design Thinking techniques from Stanford University. As students develop skills through working in the field, observing and interviewing to discover problems, and learning techniques for validating hypotheses, they learn by doing, through real world problems and collaborating with real entrepreneurs. Students work with carefully selected Singapore entrepreneurs, who present real and urgent business problems with hard deliverables and deadlines. Students learn processes including customer development, agile development, and rapid prototyping. Throughout the course, students will learn to problem solve, think critically, make well-informed decisions, communicate effectively, and engage in productive and successful team work. In the final portion of the course, students use everything they’ve learned about entrepreneurship and group work to launch their own startups. The course requires rigorous study and emphasizes in-depth research.
prerequisites: Semester I grade of B+ or higher in Humanities 9 is required to select this course in grade 10.

title: Dance I - Introduction to Dance
description: This course is designed for any student who would like to develop confidence and the ability to dance either for fun or as a performer. Students will explore a wide range of techniques and vocabulary from diverse styles, including contemporary, jazz, hip hop, and global dance forms. The curriculum emphasizes practical application, culminating in the creation of original choreography and the development of essential performance and analytical skills.
prerequisites: None

title: Racquet Sports
description: In this course, students will engage in a variety of racquet sports, such as badminton, table tennis, pickleball, and tennis. The focus is on skill development and the application of techniques within diverse sporting contexts. Teamwork, effective communication, and adherence to rules are central themes, encouraging students to develop a sense of fair play and an appreciation for lifelong fitness. While the majority of classes will take place indoors, an outdoor venue will be used periodically
prerequisites: None

title: AT Research & Catalyst
note: Students will be eligible to take the AP Research exam in preparation for earning the AP Capstone Diploma.
description: In the AT Research & Catalyst course, students continue to apply the skills learned in AT Seminar to embark on a rigorous, year-long inquiry into an academic topic of their choice. Students learn to design and execute a sophisticated qualitative, quantitative, or mixed-methods, gathering and analyzing original data to generate novel insights and contribute new knowledge to their chosen field. This independent work is supported by a network of expert mentors and enriched through experiential learning opportunities. The course concludes with a 5,000-word research paper, a formal oral defense of their thesis, and the submission of their work to a peer-reviewed academic journal. Successful completion makes students eligible for the AP Capstone diploma and fulfills the SAS Catalyst requirement.
prerequisites: AT Seminar.

title: AT Seminar
note: Students will be eligible to take the AP Seminar exam in preparation for earning the AP Capstone Diploma.
description: The AT Seminar is the first year of the AT Research & Catalyst program. This inquiry-driven course helps students develop the skills for college-level work by exploring real-world topics of their interest from multiple perspectives. Students learn to collect, analyze, and use scholarly sources to build and present evidence-based arguments both in writing and to a live audience. Since teamwork is key to research, a significant portion of the course involves collaborative work. Students submit individual papers and both individual and team presentations to the College Board. Completing AT Seminar prepares students for AT Research & Catalyst experience in the following year.
prerequisites: Semester I grade of A or higher in Humanities 9 is required to select this course in grade 10. Semester 1 grade of B+ or higher in English 10 or American Studies is required to select this course in grade 11.
